title: Finite-Basis Duality Estimate for the Surface-Code Threshold under Correlated Bit-Flip Errors
abstract: We apply finite-basis duality to a statistical-mechanical model introduced by Chubb and Flammia for the surface code under spatially correlated bit-flip noise. Their mapping gives a random-bond Ising model with both two-body edge interactions and four-body face interactions. The single-equation estimate based on the duality analysis is slightly deviated from their Monte Carlo estimate \(p_c=0.1004(6)\). Following the finite-basis and graph-polynomial strategy, we instead use periodic and twisted-periodic sectors on toroidal bases. We have obtained an improved estimate of the critical point, $p_c = 0.10348$.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical method using finite-basis duality to estimate the error threshold for the surface code under correlated noise. By systematically improving the basis size on a toroidal lattice, the authors provide a refined estimate for the critical threshold ($p_c = 0.10348$). This work advances the theoretical understanding of fault-tolerance thresholds in the presence of complex error correlations.
main_problem: The paper aims to accurately estimate the critical threshold ($p_c$) for the surface code when subjected to spatially correlated bit-flip errors.
main_result: By employing a finite-basis duality analysis on toroidal bases, the authors obtained an improved estimate for the critical point: $p_c = 0.10348$.
method: The core method involves applying finite-basis duality analysis, utilizing periodic and twisted-periodic sectors on toroidal lattices to analyze the random-bond Ising model mapping.
model_or_system: The system is the surface code under correlated bit-flip noise, which is mapped to a random-bond Ising model containing both two-body edge and four-body face interactions.
key_observables: The critical threshold ($p_c$) for error correction.
important_parameters: The error probability parameter ($p$) and the correlation structure parameter ($\eta = p_+/p_-$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on using local Boltzmann weights, quenched averages, and twisted-sector partition functions rather than a full Monte Carlo phase diagram.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the local correlated pair used by previous work and the smallest toroidal finite basis used in the current analysis. Table I compares threshold estimates using various methods (single pair, $2 	imes 2$ basis, $4 	imes 4$ basis, and MC).
paper_structure: The argument progresses by showing that the finite-basis duality method systematically refines the estimate by increasing the size of the basis used in the calculation, moving from single-pair estimates to $2 	imes 2$ and then $4 	imes 4$ finite bases.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of duality techniques to estimate critical thresholds in complex, correlated error models provides advanced theoretical tools applicable to quantum error correction codes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (Color online) (a) Local correlated pair used by Chubb and Flammia. Opposite plaquette edges e, e′ carry quenched vari- ables xe, xe′, while the statistical-mechanical Ising spins sv live on vertices. The pair gives two edge terms and a four-spin term. (b) Smallest toroidal finite basis used in Eq. (9); red and blue arcs de- note non-overlapping correlated pairs, and periodic or antiperiodic seams give the four sectors Zαβ.
